result:
Sunrise is, in my opinion, the most generous daily gift. It arrives whether or not anyone watches, spilling gold into quiet corners and tricking birds into believing the world has always been this hopeful. I like to imagine the sun stretching as it crests the horizon, shaking off a celestial drowsiness, wondering what kind of day it will energize. Some mornings feel like blank notebooks—crisp, unblemished, waiting. Others resemble a well-thumbed journal already scribbled with obligations. Either way, the light slips in, rearranging shadows, insisting on possibility.

This is usually the time when coffee begins its persuasive argument. It’s not merely a beverage but a ritualized permission to pause. The steam writes poems that disappear before you can read them. The first sip carries the earthy rumor of faraway hillsides, the second reminds you to breathe slower, and the third says, “All right, let’s begin.”

Beginnings are funny things. We treat them like rare birds, yet they flock everywhere. A hallway conversation can start a friendship. A doodle can grow into a design. A sentence, written halfheartedly, can lead to an idea that refuses to stay small. Maybe that’s why I love notebooks piled on desks, or playlists titled “In Progress,” or half-packed suitcases. They all hum with maybe.

If today’s sunrise is a reminder, it’s this: curiosity is renewable energy. Ask questions, even ordinary ones. Why does toast smell like reassurance? How many stories do raindrops carry down from clouds? What music do streetlights hum when no one listens?

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
